text: We present an unusual case of bladder mass and its laparoscopic management. A fifty-one-year-old female presented with history of irritative L U T S for last one year with no history of hematuria, tobacco use, and comorbidities. The physical examination was normal.

CT abdomen showed a heterogeneous mass in right lateral and anterior wall with calcification and hypodense area in the mass measuring four into three centimeters in size without perivesical extension and regional lymphadenopathy with normal adnexa bilaterally.

During cystoscopy, a large grayish-white mass with hair follicles and soft yellowish necrotic tissue is visible arising from the dome of the bladder. The rest of the urinary bladder and ureteric orifices appear normal.

The histopathology showed keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, presence of hair follicles, adipocytes, mucous glands in the subepithelium, with no evidence of malignancy. Therefore, final diagnosis of benign primary urinary bladder dermoid cyst was made.

The surgeon begins laparoscopic transperitoneal partial cystectomy by dropping the anterior bladder wall from the anterior abdominal wall using a harmonic scalpel, employing traction and counter traction. The procedure is performed in reverse Trendelenburg position with standard port configuration.

The surgeon carefully dissects the right ovary and fallopian tube, which are adhered to the anterior bladder wall near the dermoid cyst, using a harmonic scalpel and an endogeovascular stapler. The bladder is further separated from its lateral attachments with the harmonic scalpel, employing traction and counter traction techniques.

The surgeon excises the dermoid cystic mass located in the dome region of the bladder, maintaining a one-centimeter margin of normal bladder mucosa around the tumor. The harmonic scalpel is used for dissection, with traction and counter traction applied. Hairs protruding from the mass are visible during the excision process, which concludes with the complete removal of the tumor.

The surgeon closes the bladder wall using freehand intracorporeal suturing with three OV lock sutures, beginning at the distal aspect and progressing towards the proximal portion of the anterior bladder wall.

The surgeon visually inspects the bladder, which has been completely closed with intracorporeal sutures. The bladder is distended with 200 ml of normal saline to verify the watertight closure, showing no leakage upon distention.

The surgeon retrieves the excised specimen using an endo-catch bag and places a drain. The specimen is visually examined, revealing a well-defined grayish-brown tumor with tufts of hair and normal surrounding bladder tissue. The cut surface of the tumor shows a yellowish appearance.

The operating time was seventy minutes and estimated blood loss was forty ml. At two year follow up, patient is asymptomatic. The cystoscopy done three monthly intervals upto two years showed no tumor recurrence. CT abdomen at one year and two year follow up are normal.

So, we can conclude that if a mass with peripheral calcification with hair growth is found on anterior wall or dome of bladder, a dermoid cyst should be considered as a differential diagnosis. Primary dermoid cyst of urinary bladder can be managed by laparoscopic partial cystectomy.
